signals.name: Leopold Aschenbrenner
signals.summary: No new posts or essays found within the last 7 days. Most recent retrievable content dates to mid-2025 or earlier. His fund, Situational Awareness LP, has been widely covered for its ~270% 2026 gains and $20B AUM, but no fresh primary output from Aschenbrenner himself this week.
signals.connection: His AGI infrastructure thesis — long energy, chips, and Anthropic — is directly relevant to Anthropic's $30B run-rate story and the broader AI governance sprint, but no signal this week.

signals.name: Dan Wang
signals.summary: No new posts or X activity found in the last 7 days. Dan Wang's book 'Breakneck: China's Quest to Engineer the Future' continues to circulate and he has been doing interviews, but no fresh primary writing this week.
signals.connection: His China tech-capability analysis is highly relevant to the EU Sovereign AI push, US-China AI race, and geopolitics topics, but no signal this week.

signals.name: Nathan Benaich
signals.latest_title: Europe cannot rent its way to AI sovereignty
signals.summary: Published June 21, Benaich argues the real AI risk isn't rogue machines but that everyone outside the US and China 'rents their intelligence from a landlord who can cut them off' — illustrated by the US government ordering Anthropic to shut off its most capable model for all foreign nationals. He warns Europe is not moving fast enough to build sovereign AI capacity.
signals.connection: Directly addresses the EU Sovereign AI / EUROPA Consortium story, Anthropic's DoD legal battle and government controls, and the AI governance sprint — a rare piece that ties all three together through a European lens.

signals.name: Stanley Druckenmiller
signals.summary: No new interview or public remarks found on CNBC, Bloomberg, or FT within the last 7 days. Most recent retrievable Druckenmiller content dates to early 2026 and prior.
signals.connection: His macro views on fiscal risk, rates, and AI investment are directly relevant to the Fed rate-hike signal and Bitcoin drop stories, but no signal this week.

signals.name: Byrne Hobart
signals.latest_title: Anabasis, Memory, Height, Neo-Brandeisians, Canceled, Capacity, Compute, Realism
signals.summary: Hobart published multiple issues of The Diff this week (June 5–6 confirmed, with likely additional issues). Themes include compute capacity, automation and utilization, and software PE — consistent with his ongoing analysis of AI's economic footprint and market structure implications.
signals.connection: His compute and capacity framing connects to the Anthropic revenue surge, AI infrastructure investment, and the broader AI governance and market stories this week.

signals.name: Michael Burry
signals.summary: Posted daily on Substack through June 25. His June 12 post is a pointed AI bear case: 'LLMs are language models, not AI. No one is using AI yet,' arguing AI capital flows are punishing fundamentally sound businesses at sub-10x earnings. His June 25 post flags Hong Kong stocks falling as chip narrative rotates capital to South Korea and Japan.
signals.connection: His AI skepticism directly counters the Anthropic $30B run-rate story and frames the Bitcoin/market dislocation as driven by irrational AI capital flows — a direct counterpoint to the bullish AI narrative.

signals.name: Ben Thompson
signals.latest_title: Five Questions on WWDC 2026, Fable 5 And Its Guardrails, What Anthropic Has in Common With Apple
signals.summary: Thompson published multiple pieces this week analyzing Anthropic's 'Fable' model being shut down by Trump administration export controls, framing Anthropic's 'safety superpower' as both its strength and its strategic liability. He also covered Apple price increases and Siri AI being blocked from the EU, and Google vs. Microsoft competitive dynamics.
signals.connection: Directly covers the Anthropic DoD/legal battle angle, US AI governance sprint (export controls as policy tool), EU AI sovereignty, and Apple Intelligence — hitting four of today's top story areas in one week.

signals.name: Paul Graham
signals.latest_title: How to Earn a Billion Dollars
signals.summary: Published June 14, Graham released a new essay drawn from an Oxford Union talk laying out the mechanics of building billion-dollar wealth through startups, arguing wealth creation is not zero-sum and that the patterns apply even to non-founders such as future politicians. The piece went viral with 2.3M views on X.
signals.connection: Tangentially relevant to the AI startup ecosystem and capital formation themes; less directly tied to this week's geopolitical or regulatory stories, though his framing of founder leverage resonates with AI lab valuations like Anthropic's.

signals.name: Benedict Evans
signals.latest_title: AI eats the world (Spring 2026 deck, updated May 2026)
signals.summary: Evans's X account is locked (approved followers only), limiting direct post retrieval. His May 2026 macro deck 'AI eats the world' remains the most recent substantive output, and his essay on OpenAI's strategic gaps (May 24) questioned whether OpenAI has unique tech, a sticky user base, or a clear plan given incumbents matching it on capability.
signals.connection: His OpenAI analysis connects to the Anthropic revenue surge story — if Anthropic is at $30B run-rate while OpenAI lacks a clear moat, Evans's strategic framework becomes highly relevant to the frontier AI governance and competitive landscape.

signals.name: Packy McCormick
signals.latest_title: Westmag: Why America Needs a Motor and Actuator Company
signals.summary: On June 2, McCormick promoted his deep-dive on Westmag, an American motor and actuator company he invested in, arguing the US needs domestic capability in these physical components. No major new Not Boring essay found in the last 7 days.
signals.connection: The Westmag piece connects to industrial competitiveness and supply chain themes adjacent to the US-China geopolitics and defense/security topic areas; light relevance to this week's AI governance stories.

signals.name: CJ Gustafson
signals.summary: No substantive new Mostly Metrics posts or X activity found within the last 7 days. Recent LinkedIn activity shows him participating in an Abacum webinar on AI-first FP&A, but no fresh primary written content this week.
signals.connection: His SaaS metrics and CFO-lens content would connect to Anthropic's revenue trajectory and the broader AI impact on software business models, but no signal this week.

signals.name: Lenny Rachitsky
signals.summary: On June 4, Rachitsky reacted to Anthropic's announcement that its engineers ship 8x as much code per quarter as they did in 2021–2025, commenting 'Wild.' His June 23 pinned post promoted a podcast episode on how the biggest workplace upheaval of our lifetime is underway, featuring OpenAI's executive coach.
signals.connection: His reaction to Anthropic's productivity stat directly ties to the Anthropic revenue/growth story and the Labor & Workforce topic; the OpenAI coaching podcast connects to AI's impact on work and talent.

signals.name: Andrej Karpathy
signals.latest_title: Claude as an org-level team entity (third paradigm thread)
signals.summary: On June 23, Karpathy posted a widely discussed thread (6.1M views) proposing a 'third paradigm' for Claude: not a personal assistant or Slack bot, but an org-wide harness where AI writes the majority of code, is deeply integrated, and makes everyone feel like a manager. He defended Anthropic's approach against criticism and noted the distinction from simple agent frameworks.
signals.connection: Directly relevant to Anthropic's $30B run-rate story, the AI governance and applied tech topics, and the labor/workforce transformation theme — Karpathy's framing of 'everyone is a manager' is the clearest articulation of how AI reshapes enterprise work.

signals.name: Ilya Sutskever
signals.summary: No new posts found within the last 7 days. Most recent public X post dates to February 2026 (supporting Anthropic's stance on not backing down from safety obligations). SSI continues to operate under his formal CEO role after Daniel Gross's departure in July 2025.
signals.connection: SSI's safe superintelligence mission is adjacent to the AI governance and frontier AI topics, but no signal this week.

signals.name: Noam Brown
signals.summary: No new posts found within the last 7 days. His @NoamBrown handle has only 5 posts total and is inactive; @polynoamial has 1,671 posts but no recent content was retrievable in this window.
signals.connection: His reasoning and multi-agent AI research at OpenAI is relevant to the frontier AI topic area, but no signal this week.

signals.name: François Chollet
signals.summary: On June 22, Chollet pushed back against 'SaaS bears' claiming AI makes all software worthless, arguing the claim shows 'staggering short-sightedness' — AI-generated code benefits software developers most, code is not the product, and easier code means more software and more SaaS usage. On June 15, he posted that 'opaque and arbitrary regulatory strikes are counter-productive for the whole industry' even for those who favor AI regulation.
signals.connection: His June 15 post on arbitrary regulatory strikes speaks directly to the Anthropic/Fable export control episode and the AI governance sprint; his SaaS take connects to the Applied Tech & Industry and Markets topic areas.
